I would say that almost every interaction with the user (such as this one) is very likely a moment where the system should learn about the user's perspective, or about the product that it's being built. All I can think of that is NOT a lesson worth persisting is a direct instruction like "git pull; the remote has moved on" or something like that -- but for example if the user says "hey, when I click on an avatar it doesn't take me to the user's profile" and that was not part of the product, then clearly the user is manifesting a new (or buggy) capability of the product and should likely be aggregated as part of the product spec wiki.

Thoughts?
assistant

Agreed that capture is too narrowly scoped toward assistant corrections; proposed adding product-spec categories, feeding triage the wiki index so NO means 'transient or already-known,' and investing in supersession logic for the merge step rather than just widening the YES gate.

it's a positive -- imagine; if you grabbed only the wiki and dumped it on a new project and tried to one-shot the app, that level of detail, organized, should be all the nuance that's captured.

The guiding principle is "human time is irreplaceable, tokens are buyable"

one important bit is that if the user says "yes" to something the model says, quoting the user as saying "yes" is meaningiless -- your take on how to navigate that
assistant

Distinguished content (what the spec says) from authorization (whether it's real): a bare 'yes' carries no content, so the quote should reference the model's proposal with an affirmed flag, and model-authored content requires human affirmation to become spec.
